topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the glossy brochure lying on his desk. The typeface was sleek, the promises of 'synergistic growth' shimmering under the fluorescent lights of the office. His palms began to sweat, a cold dampness seeping through his thin shirt. He’d been tasked with updating the quarterly reports, a mundane job, but now, the reality of the situation—that OmniCorp was up for sale—felt like a physical blow. He quickly shoved the brochure under a stack of archived memos, his movements jerky. When his manager, Sarah, walked by, Liam forced a bright, unnaturally loud laugh, the sound grating in his own ears. "Just catching up on some legacy data," he mumbled, trying to appear busy, acutely aware of the silence that followed his performance.

[story 2]

“So, the rumors are true,” Mark muttered, running a hand over his closely cropped hair. He kept glancing toward the conference room where the executive team was supposedly meeting, his gaze darting nervously. The air in the open-plan office suddenly felt thick, suffocating. He felt a distinct tightening in his chest, like a knot being slowly cinched tight. He tried to focus on his monitor, clicking rapidly through spreadsheets, but his fingers kept shaking. When his colleague, David, leaned over to ask a question, Mark flinched, pulling back as if burned. "Yeah, whatever," he clipped out, his voice several octaves higher than normal, desperate to disappear into the beige cubicle wall.

[story 3]

Eleanor found herself standing by the water cooler, pretending to examine a water stain on the wall. Every glance toward the main entrance seemed to draw unwanted attention. A sudden wave of heat washed over her face, making her cheeks flush a deep crimson. She clutched her lukewarm mug so tightly her knuckles turned white. The whispers, faint but distinct, seemed to follow her footsteps as she walked toward the breakroom. When her supervisor, Mr. Henderson, approached, Eleanor managed a strained smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach her eyes. "Just contemplating the future, sir," she managed, the words tasting like ash in her mouth, acutely aware of how transparent her attempt at nonchalance had become.
